stem: Using Fermat's Little Theorem, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is divided by the prime \( [[p]] \).
solution: By Fermat's Little Theorem \( [[a]]^{[[p]]-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{[[p]]} \). Writing \( [[b]] = ([[p]]-1)q + r \) reduces the exponent to \( r = [[b]] \bmod ([[p]]-1) \), so \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv [[a]]^{r} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[p]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), overshooting the reduced exponent by one.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), undershooting by one.
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[p]] \), as if the exponent reduced to 1.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is three more than the correct remainder.
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[p]] \), as if the exponent reduced to 2.

stem: Using Fermat's Little Theorem, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[k]]!} \) is divided by the prime \( [[p]] \).
solution: First compute the exponent itself: \( [[k]]! \). By Fermat's Little Theorem \( [[a]]^{[[p]]-1}\equiv 1\pmod{[[p]]} \), so only \( [[k]]! \bmod ([[p]]-1) \) matters; evaluating gives \( [[a]]^{[[k]]!} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[p]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the un-evaluated exponent \( [[k]] \) instead of \( [[k]]! \).
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[p]] \), as if the exponent reduced to 1.
This reduces the exponent \( [[k]]! \) itself mod \( [[p]] \) instead of using it as a power of \( [[a]] \).
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is three more than the correct remainder.
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[p]] \), as if the exponent reduced to 2.

stem: Using Fermat's Little Theorem on each term, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} + [[c]]^{[[d]]} \) is divided by the prime \( [[p]] \).
solution: Reduce each term separately by Fermat's Little Theorem, \( [[a]]^{[[p]]-1}\equiv1 \) and \( [[c]]^{[[p]]-1}\equiv1\pmod{[[p]]} \), then add the residues and reduce mod \( [[p]] \): the remainder is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only reduces the first term, dropping the second entirely.
This only reduces the second term, dropping the first entirely.
This multiplies the two reduced terms instead of adding them.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is three more than the correct remainder.
This uses one extra power on the second term.

stem: Fermat's Little Theorem also holds in the unconditional form \( a^{p} \equiv a \pmod{p} \), true for EVERY integer \( a \) (even multiples of \( p \)), not just those coprime to \( p \). What is \( [[a]]^{[[p]]} \bmod [[p]] \)?
solution: By the unconditional form of Fermat's Little Theorem, \( [[a]]^{[[p]]} \equiv [[a]] \pmod{[[p]]} \) regardless of whether \( [[p]] \) divides \( [[a]] \). Reducing \( [[a]] \) itself modulo \( [[p]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the coprime-only exponent \( p-1 \), which requires \( \gcd(a,p)=1 \) and is not what this exponent equals.
This is \( [[a]] \) itself, unreduced, rather than \( [[a]] \bmod [[p]] \).
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[p]] \), the wrong exponent.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is one less than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is the complementary residue \( p - \text{answer} \), the wrong sign class.

stem: Using Fermat's Little Theorem on each factor, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \cdot [[c]]^{[[d]]} \) is divided by the prime \( [[p]] \).
solution: Reduce each factor separately by Fermat's Little Theorem, \( [[a]]^{[[p]]-1}\equiv1 \) and \( [[c]]^{[[p]]-1}\equiv1\pmod{[[p]]} \), then multiply the residues and reduce mod \( [[p]] \): the remainder is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only reduces the first factor, dropping the second entirely.
This only reduces the second factor, dropping the first entirely.
This adds the two reduced factors instead of multiplying them.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is three more than the correct remainder.
This uses one extra power on the second factor.
